questions.q: What is the difference between an app in the foreground and an app in the background on iOS?
options: Foreground apps are actively visible and receive user input; background apps are suspended and cannot execute code except for specific allowed tasks
Foreground apps use more battery; background apps have no power consumption
Both foreground and background apps execute code at the same rate
Background apps are partially visible behind other apps
questions.explanation: Foreground apps run normally and respond to input; background apps are suspended to conserve resources. Background execution is limited to specific modes like audio playback or location updates, declared in Info.plist. A misconception is that background apps stop instantly.

questions.q: In iOS, what happens when a user force-quits an app from the App Switcher?
options: The app is terminated immediately without calling willTerminate(); state is not saved automatically
The app gracefully shuts down and saves all data to disk
The app enters the background and can continue running
Force-quit is not possible in iOS
questions.explanation: Force-quitting bypasses the normal lifecycle and does not trigger willTerminate() or other cleanup. Apps must save state in viewDidDisappear() or background modes to ensure data persistence. A misconception is that willTerminate() always executes.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the onPause() lifecycle method in Android?
options: Called when the Activity loses focus but remains visible; used to pause animations or stop resource-intensive operations
Called when the Activity is being destroyed and removed from memory
Called when the Activity is first created and initialized
Called when the device screen is rotated
questions.explanation: onPause() is invoked when the Activity is partially obscured but still visible. It is the appropriate place to pause media playback or release camera access. A misconception is that onPause() means the Activity is gone. It persists until onStop() or onDestroy() is called.

questions.q: How should an app handle temporary interruptions like an incoming call on Android?
options: Save state in onPause() so that if the system kills the app while the call is active, state can be restored via savedInstanceState
Assume the app will always be restored and do not save state
Prevent the call from interrupting the app
Interruptions are automatic and require no app logic
questions.explanation: Temporary interruptions trigger onPause() but may not trigger onDestroy(). The system might kill the app to free memory, so saving state ensures recovery. A misconception is that onPause() means the user is done. They may return after the interruption.

questions.q: What is the sceneDelegate in iOS, and how does it relate to the app lifecycle?
options: Manages multiple windows and scenes in multitasking environments; handles scene connection and disconnection events for modern iOS apps
Manages the app delegate's networking requests
Handles device memory warnings
Manages background task execution
questions.explanation: SceneDelegate (iOS 13+) separates window/scene management from app state, enabling multi-window and split-view support. AppDelegate handles global app lifecycle; SceneDelegate handles per-scene lifecycle. A misconception is that AppDelegate is obsolete.

questions.q: In Android, what happens if an Activity calls finish() during onCreate()?
options: The Activity immediately calls onDestroy() and is removed from the back stack without displaying to the user
The Activity displays briefly before being destroyed
finish() cannot be called from onCreate()
The Activity is paused but not destroyed
questions.explanation: Calling finish() at any lifecycle stage triggers onDestroy() and removes the Activity from the stack. If called during onCreate(), the user never sees the Activity. A misconception is that calling finish() is graceful. It bypasses onResume() and prevents normal state saving.

questions.q: What is the purpose of savedInstanceState in Android, and when is it restored?
options: A Bundle that saves non-permanent state across Activity recreation due to configuration changes; restored in onCreate() or onRestoreInstanceState()
A permanent storage mechanism for app data across sessions
An in-memory cache that persists between app launches
A security mechanism for protecting sensitive user data
questions.explanation: savedInstanceState preserves UI state during temporary destruction (rotation, language change). It is not suitable for large data or permanent storage. A misconception is that savedInstanceState is reliable for permanent data. It can be discarded if memory is scarce.

questions.q: How should background tasks be handled in iOS to avoid termination?
options: Use Background App Refresh or Background Modes (audio, location, VoIP) declared in Info.plist; utilize background tasks like beginBackgroundTask()
Apps can run indefinitely in the background without restrictions
Background execution is not possible in iOS
Use a background thread and the app will automatically keep running
questions.explanation: iOS severely restricts background execution. Apps must declare specific Background Modes and use the Background Task API for grace periods. Undeclared background activity may cause termination. A misconception is that multithreading allows infinite background execution.

questions.q: What is the difference between app termination and app suspension in iOS?
options: Suspension pauses execution and the app can resume; termination removes the app from memory and requires a full restart
Suspension removes the app; termination is temporary
Both are identical processes
Termination is user-initiated; suspension is automatic
questions.explanation: Suspended apps remain in memory but do not execute code; terminated apps are purged and must be relaunched. The system may terminate suspended apps to reclaim memory. A misconception is that terminated apps alert the system to save state.

questions.q: How should memory warnings be handled in Android and iOS?
options: Android: override onTrimMemory(); iOS: respond to memoryWarning notifications; both should release non-critical objects to avoid termination
Memory warnings are informational and do not require response
Apps should ignore memory warnings and rely on the system to manage memory
Memory warnings only occur on low-end devices
questions.explanation: Memory warnings indicate the system is under pressure; apps that ignore them risk termination. Clearing caches, closing unused views, and releasing large objects are appropriate responses. A misconception is that only poor apps receive warnings. Even well-designed apps may trigger warnings under heavy use.
